label: ldap3 conn.entries vs conn.response

entry.description: conn.entries returns a list of ldap3 Entry objects with an ORM-style interface (entry.cn.value), not plain dicts. Subscripting them with ["attributes"]["cn"] raises a TypeError at runtime against a real directory. conn.response is the raw list of dicts where entry["attributes"]["cn"] works as expected. Filter conn.response to e.get("type") == "searchResEntry" to strip referrals and the result-done control entry.
entry.comparison: conn.entries provides ORM-style access; conn.response provides raw dict access. The bug is invisible in unit tests that mock the LDAP connection and only surfaces against a live directory.
use_cases: Filtering searchResEntry results from LDAP responses

errors: use_cases: Input should be a valid string

label: moto e2e tests — rewiring module-level boto3 clients

entry.tool: moto e2e tests — rewiring module-level boto3 clients
entry.description: boto3.client() called at module import time binds to real AWS before @mock_aws activates. To fix this, reassign the module's client attributes in setUp inside the moto context. SSM caches also survive between tests and must be manually busted. AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED=true must be set before importing any module that calls boto3.client() at the top level, otherwise the import hangs in CI trying to reach the EC2 metadata endpoint.
entry.comparison: Module-level boto3 clients bind before moto context is active; reassigning in setUp ensures they bind within the mock context.
use_cases: Testing Lambda functions with mocked AWS services

errors: use_cases: Input should be a valid string

label: DynamoDB item key names must match KeySchema

entry.tool: DynamoDB item key names must match KeySchema
entry.description: The audit log helper wrote items with key request_id matching the table's KeySchema, but tests asserted record["username"] causing a KeyError that only appeared when the DynamoDB scan ran against moto. The actual item had request_id, employee_name, timestamp, status, and ttl. Always derive test assertions from the actual write path, not assumptions about naming.
entry.comparison: Assumed field names in tests vs actual field names from write path can diverge silently until running against a real or mocked DynamoDB.

errors: use_cases: Input should be a valid string

label: SQS dead-letter queue for async Lambda invocations

entry.tool: SQS dead-letter queue for async Lambda invocations
entry.description: When a Lambda is invoked with InvocationType="Event" (async), AWS retries it twice on failure before discarding the event. Attaching a dead_letter_config with an SQS queue ARN captures the failed payload after those retries. The Lambda execution role needs sqs:SendMessage on the DLQ ARN, and if the Lambda runs inside a VPC, an SQS VPC interface endpoint is required.
entry.comparison: Without a DLQ, failed async Lambda invocations are silently discarded after two retries. With a DLQ, failed payloads are preserved for inspection and reprocessing.

errors: use_cases: Input should be a valid string

label: CloudWatch metric alarm on SQS ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible

entry.tool: CloudWatch metric alarm on SQS ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible
entry.description: ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible > 0 with period = 60 and evaluation_periods = 1 gives a 60-second detection window on a DLQ. treat_missing_data = "notBreaching" keeps the alarm green when the queue is empty rather than entering INSUFFICIENT_DATA. Wiring alarm_actions to an SNS topic closes the loop so the same pipeline that sends notifications also alerts on its own failures.
entry.comparison: Without an alarm, DLQ messages can accumulate unnoticed. With the alarm, failures surface within 60 seconds.
use_cases: CloudWatch alarm configuration for AWS pipelines

errors: use_cases: Input should be a valid string

label: API Gateway HTTP API (v2) throttling

entry.description: HTTP API stages support default_route_settings with throttling_burst_limit (token bucket peak) and throttling_rate_limit (sustained req/s). This applies to all routes unless overridden per-route. A valid API key does not bypass throttling — the authorizer runs first, then throttling applies. Exceeding the limit returns a 429 Too Many Requests before the Lambda is ever invoked.
entry.comparison: Per-stage throttling applies globally to all routes; per-route overrides allow finer-grained control. Throttling is enforced after authorization but before Lambda invocation.
use_cases: API Gateway rate limiting with Terraform
Protecting Lambda backends from traffic spikes
Multi-route throttle configuration in AWS HTTP APIs

errors: use_cases: Input should be a valid string

label: IAM least-privilege — scoping Bedrock to a specific model ARN

entry.tool: IAM least-privilege — scoping Bedrock to a specific model ARN
entry.description: Bedrock foundation model ARNs follow the format arn:aws:bedrock:<region>::foundation-model/<model-id> with a double colon (no account ID, as foundation models are AWS-managed resources). Scoping bedrock:InvokeModel to this ARN instead of "*" means the role cannot call any other model, even if new ones are added to the account. The ARN must be updated if the model is switched.
entry.comparison: Using "*" for bedrock:InvokeModel grants access to all foundation models. Scoping to a specific ARN enforces least-privilege and prevents unintended model access.
use_cases: Security hardening for AI workloads on AWS

errors: use_cases: Input should be a valid string

label: Resolving AWS account ID in Terraform without hardcoding

entry.tool: Resolving AWS account ID in Terraform without hardcoding
entry.description: data "aws_caller_identity" "current" {} resolves the account ID of whoever is running terraform apply. Use data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id in ARNs to avoid hardcoding a 12-digit account number. This is the standard pattern for IAM resource ARNs that include the account ID (Lambda, SNS, DynamoDB, etc.) where a direct resource reference would create a circular dependency.
entry.comparison: Hardcoded account IDs break portability across accounts and environments. Using aws_caller_identity is dynamic and avoids circular dependency issues.
use_cases: Avoiding circular dependencies in Terraform resource ARNs

errors: use_cases: Input should be a valid string

label: Exponential backoff retry pattern for external connections

entry.tool: Exponential backoff retry pattern for external connections
entry.description: Retry loops should distinguish between transient failures (network timeout, connection refused) and permanent failures (wrong credentials, operation not permitted). The pattern: loop up to N attempts, check str(e).lower() for auth error strings and raise immediately, sleep base * (2 ** (attempt - 1)) between retries, re-raise the last exception after exhausting attempts. Import time inside the function to avoid patching it globally in tests.
entry.comparison: Flat retry intervals waste time and can trigger lockouts on permanent failures. Exponential backoff with failure-type discrimination is more efficient and safer.
use_cases: Resilient LDAP or API connections in Python
Retry logic for transient AWS service errors
Avoiding account lockouts on auth failures

errors: use_cases: Input should be a valid string

label: SSM Parameter Store as a runtime-tunable config store with Lambda caching

entry.tool: SSM Parameter Store as a runtime-tunable config store with Lambda caching
entry.description: Reading SSM on every Lambda invocation adds 10–50 ms latency and costs $0.05 per 10,000 reads. The standard pattern is to cache the value in a module-level dict with a TTL timestamp. On invocation, check if time.time() - loaded_at > TTL; if stale, fetch and update. A 5-minute TTL means config changes propagate within one cache window without redeployment. Multiple independent parameters should have separate cache dicts. Tests must manually reset the cache dict between test cases.
entry.comparison: No caching: low latency variance but high cost and latency per invocation. TTL-based caching: reduces cost and latency with a bounded propagation delay for config changes.
use_cases: Cost optimization for SSM reads in high-frequency Lambda functions

errors: use_cases: Input should be a valid string

label: Python test patch targets — import namespace matters

entry.tool: Python test patch targets — import namespace matters
entry.description: Patching with @patch("module_a.some_func") patches the name in module_a's namespace. If module_b does from module_a import some_func, the binding in module_b is already made at import time and patching module_a.some_func has no effect on calls inside module_b. You must patch module_b.some_func to affect calls inside module_b. The rule: patch where the name is used, not where it is defined.
entry.comparison: Patching at the definition location does not affect already-bound references in other modules. Patching at the usage location correctly intercepts calls.
use_cases: Testing modules that import functions from other modules

errors: use_cases: Input should be a valid string

label: AWS Simple AD vs Managed Microsoft AD

entry.tool: AWS Simple AD vs Managed Microsoft AD
entry.description: Simple AD (~$36–40/month Small) is Samba 4-based and lacks trust relationships, MFA, and PowerShell AD module support. Managed Microsoft AD Standard (~$87–140/month) provides a true Microsoft AD domain controller. For workloads that only perform basic LDAP operations (user create, group membership modify, account disable via userAccountControl), Simple AD is functionally identical at less than half the cost.
entry.comparison: Simple AD: Samba 4-based, cheaper, lacks trusts/MFA/PowerShell AD. Managed Microsoft AD: true AD domain controller, more expensive, full feature set. Choose based on whether missing features are actually needed.
use_cases: Basic user and group management via LDAP
Architecture decisions for AWS directory services

errors: use_cases: Input should be a valid string

label: GitHub Actions — Node.js runtime deprecation for actions

entry.tool: GitHub Actions — Node.js runtime deprecation for actions
entry.description: GitHub Actions runs each uses: action on a Node.js runtime. When GitHub deprecates a Node version (e.g. Node 20 to Node 24 cutover June 2026), actions pinned to older major versions emit warnings and will eventually fail. The fix is to bump the action version tag: actions/checkout@v4 to @v5, actions/setup-python@v5 to @v6. Check the action's releases page to confirm the new tag ships with the required Node runtime.
entry.comparison: Older action version tags use deprecated Node runtimes and will fail after cutover dates. Newer version tags ship with updated Node runtimes and remain supported.
use_cases: Proactive deprecation handling for automated workflows
